title: Milk bread buns
subtitle: Pillowy soft buns using the tangzhong water roux method
excerpt: Individual soft white buns made with a milk and water roux (tangzhong) incorporated into an enriched dough. The roux makes these buns significantly softer and longer-lasting than a standard enriched roll.
sourceNotes: The tangzhong technique (water roux) applied to individual buns is the same method used for shokupan. The bun form became popular outside Japan through Taiwanese bakeries in the twentieth century and has spread widely into English-language domestic baking. The technique is documented in Yvonne Chen's 65°C Tangzhong Bread (2007), which codified the specific temperature and method for English-language audiences.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze baked and cooled. Reheat from frozen at 180°C for 8 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Stays fresh for 3 days wrapped at room temperature.

glossaryTerms.definition: A water roux made by cooking flour with water or milk to around 65°C until the starch gelatinises into a thick paste. Added to bread dough, it traps extra moisture in the crumb and produces buns that are significantly softer and stay fresh longer than those made without it.

content.text: adds 5-10 minutes to the prep but produces a noticeably different result: buns that compress to almost nothing and spring back, with a cloud-like interior that doesn't stale quickly. The method is the same as for shokupan — a small flour-and-milk paste cooked to 65°C and cooled before being incorporated into the main dough. Buns are shaped individually and proved touching on a tray so they pull apart at the table.

Whisk 25 g {{strong-white-flour}}, 100 ml {{whole-milk}}, and 25 ml {{water}} in a small saucepan until smooth. Cook over medium-low heat, stirring constantly, until it thickens to a glossy paste that leaves a trail when you draw a line through it (about 65°C). Cool to room temperature.
Mix, knead, and bulk ferment, 1 hour

Combine 315 g {{strong-white-flour}}, {{fast-action-yeast}}, {{fine-salt}}, and {{caster-sugar}}. Add the cooled tangzhong, lukewarm {{whole-milk}}, and {{whole-eggs}}. Mix to a rough dough, then add the {{unsalted-butter}} and knead for 10-12 minutes by hand or 7-8 minutes in a stand mixer until smooth and very elastic. The dough should be soft and slightly tacky but come away from the sides of the bowl. Cover and leave to bulk ferment until doubled, about 1 hour.

Line a baking tray with baking paper. Divide the dough into 12 equal pieces. Shape each into a tight ball by pulling the surface down and pinching underneath. Arrange in a 3 x 4 grid on the lined tray, touching. Cover with oiled cling film and prove until doubled and the buns are visibly puffy and touching, about 45 minutes.

Heat the oven to 190°C (170°C fan). Brush the buns gently with milk or beaten egg. Bake for 16-18 minutes until deep golden. Cool on the tray for 5 minutes, then transfer to a rack. Serve warm or at room temperature.
